decision_simulation: The real historical moment: 1868, Spain, immediately after La Gloriosa forced Isabella II into exile. The revolutionary government must decide what to put on the new national coinage — using a monarch's portrait risked re-legitimizing exactly the kind of personal royal power the revolution had just overthrown, but abandoning royal portraiture entirely was a radical break from centuries of Spanish coinage tradition. The outcome was genuinely uncertain: no one knew yet what kind of government Spain would become, or whether the monarchy would even stay abolished (Amadeo I would in fact be invited to become king just three years later, in 1871).Player choice: Use an allegorical figure (Hispania) instead of any monarch's portrait, or wait to select a new king first.Historical resolution: The revolutionary government chose the allegorical "Hispania" design in 1869 — a currency without a monarch's face, even though the monarchy itself would return briefly in 1871 with Amadeo I.

understand.economic_impact: Spain's transition from the Franco dictatorship (ending 1975) to a modern eurozone-ready economy in roughly twenty-five years required building institutional currency credibility essentially from scratch [TH Script Writing Bible voice profile]. The Spanish Civil War's dual-currency period created severe economic fragmentation, with two competing monetary systems operating in the same country simultaneously Wikimedia Commons: Local banknotes of the Spanish Civil War.
understand.political_impact: The peseta's creation was a direct act of political erasure — replacing a deposed queen's portrait with an allegorical figure just months after her exile [master bible coin design data]. Its journey through five distinct regimes (Savoy monarchy, First Republic, Bourbon Restoration, Second Republic, Franco dictatorship, and constitutional monarchy) makes it one of the clearest single-currency records of a nation's turbulent 20th-century political history Wikipedia: Spanish peseta.
understand.why_it_still_matters: When Spain kicked out its queen in 1868, the new government had to decide what picture to put on their money instead of a royal face — and they chose an imaginary woman instead of a new king, because nobody was sure yet what kind of country Spain would become. Over the next hundred years, that same currency watched Spain cycle through kings, republics, a civil war fought with two different kinds of money at once, and a forty-year dictatorship, before finally becoming a stable modern democracy in just twenty-five years. Carlos's story matters because it shows that a country's money can be a mirror of everything chaotic happening around it, and that a nation can rebuild trust in itself — and its currency — remarkably fast, if it decides to.
